pre_training.title: Pre-Training Nutrition
pre_training.advice: Eat a balanced meal 2-3 hours before training with complex carbs, lean protein, and healthy fats. Examples: oatmeal with banana and peanut butter, chicken with brown rice.
tips: Stay hydrated — drink 500ml water 2 hours before
Avoid heavy or greasy foods within 1 hour of training
A small snack (banana, energy bar) 30 min before is fine

post_training.title: Post-Training Recovery
post_training.advice: Consume protein and carbs within 30-60 minutes after training to maximize recovery. Aim for a 3:1 carb-to-protein ratio.
tips: Protein shake with banana is a quick option
Chocolate milk is an excellent recovery drink
Rehydrate with electrolyte-rich drinks after intense sessions

hydration.title: Hydration Guidelines
hydration.advice: Drink at least 2-3 liters of water daily. Increase intake during training days. Monitor urine color — pale yellow indicates good hydration.
tips: Add electrolytes for sessions longer than 60 minutes
Avoid excessive caffeine which can cause dehydration

muscle_building.title: Muscle Building Diet
muscle_building.advice: Consume 1.6-2.2g protein per kg of body weight daily. Eat in a slight caloric surplus (300-500 kcal above maintenance).
tips: Include leucine-rich foods: eggs, chicken, dairy, legumes
Don't skip carbs — they fuel your workouts

weight_management.title: Weight Management
weight_management.advice: For cutting, aim for a 300-500 calorie deficit. Keep protein high (2g/kg) to preserve muscle mass while losing fat.
tips: Track calories for at least 2 weeks to understand your baseline
Prioritize whole foods over processed alternatives
Eat slowly and mindfully to recognize satiety cues

cricket.match_day: High-carb breakfast (porridge, toast, fruit). Light lunch with lean protein. Snack between sessions: energy bars, bananas, dried fruits.
cricket.training: Balanced meals with emphasis on sustained energy. Include iron-rich foods for endurance.
cricket.supplements: Creatine for explosive power, protein powder for recovery, vitamin D for bone health.

badminton.match_day: Light, easily digestible meals. Focus on quick-release carbs for fast energy. Avoid dairy close to matches.
badminton.training: Moderate carb intake. Emphasize foods supporting joint health: fatty fish, nuts, turmeric.
badminton.supplements: Omega-3 for joint health, magnesium for muscle function, BCAAs for recovery.

basketball.match_day: High-carb meal 3 hours before. Energy gels or sports drinks during halftime. Post-game protein within 30 minutes.
basketball.training: Focus on lean protein and complex carbs. Include calcium-rich foods for bone density.
basketball.supplements: Protein powder, creatine monohydrate, electrolyte mixes.

volleyball.match_day: Moderate carb loading. Stay hydrated. Light protein with each meal. Avoid high-fiber foods on match days.
volleyball.training: Balanced macros with emphasis on shoulder and joint-supporting nutrients.
volleyball.supplements: Collagen for joint health, vitamin C, protein powder.

football.match_day: Carb-loading the night before. Pre-match meal: pasta, rice, or toast with lean protein 3 hours prior.
football.training: High calorie intake to support intense training. Focus on iron, B-vitamins, and antioxidants.

tennis.match_day: Easily digestible carbs. Frequent small snacks during changeovers: bananas, energy chews. Sip sports drinks.
tennis.training: Anti-inflammatory foods: berries, leafy greens, fatty fish. Moderate carb, high protein.
tennis.supplements: Turmeric/curcumin, omega-3, magnesium for cramp prevention.

athletics.match_day: Sport-specific: sprinters need more protein, distance runners need more carbs. Time meals carefully around events.
athletics.training: Periodize nutrition with training cycles. Higher carbs on high-volume days, higher protein on recovery days.
athletics.supplements: Iron (especially for female athletes), vitamin D, beet root juice for endurance.
